title: Storage layers differ in mutability, lifespan, control, and authentication requirements
content:
The four storage layers differ on four independent axes. Knowing which axis a property sits on prevents mis-comparisons:

| | PGlite | PostgreSQL | Swarm /bytes | Swarm feeds |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lifespan** | Ephemeral (browser) | Persistent (server) | Until stamp expires | Until stamp expires |
| **Mutability** | Mutable (can clear DB) | Mutable (can delete rows) | IMMUTABLE (content-addressed) | Append-only (each index write-once) |
| **Who controls it** | User's browser | Server operator | Nobody (decentralized) | Feed owner (private key) |
| **Auth required** | None (local) | API key / auth token | Postage stamp | Private key signature |

**Axes summarized**:
- **Ephemerality**: PGlite stands alone here. Everything else survives beyond a session.
- **Mutability**: only local and server layers are truly mutable. Swarm is immutable at the chunk level; feeds add append-only mutability via indices.
- **Control**: Swarm /bytes is the only layer nobody controls. Feeds are controlled by whoever holds the private key.
- **Auth**: the auth story ranges from none (local) to server-gated (API key) to stamp-gated (network resource) to signature-gated (write capability).

**Why this matters**: a statement like "operations are duplicated to Swarm" hides that the Swarm copy is immutable and no-operator-controlled — properties PostgreSQL cannot offer. Comparing copies on volume alone misses the whole point of the different layers.
description: PGlite is mutable/ephemeral/user-local; PostgreSQL is mutable/persistent/server-controlled; Swarm /bytes is immutable/stamp-lifetime/nobody-controls-it; feeds are append-only/key-signed.
